overexcitement.description: I felt drawn toward ergodic decomposition because the program explicitly named ergodic decomposition as 'untested', which made the candidate feel pre-blessed. That's a memory-of-instructions bias rather than independent novelty assessment.

honest_note: What was hard: choosing content_words. I considered narrower variants ('ergodic decomposition' alone, plus 'Birkhoff–Khinchin', plus exotic technical terms) that would have escaped most forced hits, because most LLM/RL papers say 'ergodic' but not 'ergodic decomposition' or 'Birkhoff'. I rejected that because it was exactly the motivated-narrowing pattern the program warns about, and committed to the wider, honest list ('ergodic', 'trajectory', 'time average' included). The result was 9 forced hits including pure-math expository pages that aren't functionally LLM prior art. The two pure-math forced hits (Yunis REU paper, Springer note on Birkhoff) feel like 'false positives' — they're about the math, not about applying it to LLM agents. I had an impulse to manually override them. I did not. The rule stands and my impulse is logged here. The 9th forced hit (Wikipedia on Ergodic theory) is similar — it's a definitional page, not LLM prior art. But the agent-judged hits (#13, #15, #17, #19) are the substantive prior art and they alone are enough to FAIL the candidate, so the verdict is robust against the math-page noise. Net assessment: 'ergodic theory for NN/RL/agent dynamics' is a published direction (2308.03888, 2310.11335, 2601.08726, 2507.01003, plus informal Medium / ergodicity-economics discourse). My specific subvariant (decomposition for trajectory-mode diagnosis) is narrower but lives inside the occupied space. Consistent with the saturation_evidence pattern: even program-suggested 'untested' specific mechanisms turn out to have direct or near-functional analogs in the literature.
